_meta.authoringNotes: Content structured as a parent-injection condition covering the full mood disorder spectrum per DSM-5. Citations cross-verified against APA Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders 5th Ed., NIMH mood disorder resources, and PubMed-indexed meta-analyses.

h1Title: Mood Disorders: Symptoms, Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Mood disorders are a group of mental health conditions characterized by persistent or recurring disturbances in emotional state that significantly impair thinking, behavior, and daily functioning. They span a spectrum from major depressive disorder and persistent depressive disorder on one end, through bipolar I, bipolar II, and cyclothymic disorder on the other, to contextually triggered conditions such as seasonal affective disorder, premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and substance-induced mood disorder. Mood disorders affect hundreds of millions of people worldwide and are among the leading causes of disability, yet they remain underdiagnosed and undertreated. Effective treatments including psychotherapy, pharmacotherapy, and neuromodulation exist across the full spectrum, and evidence-based care can produce substantial and lasting recovery. Understanding the distinctions between conditions on this spectrum, recognizing early warning signs, and accessing appropriate care are the critical first steps toward management.

keyStats.prevalence: Depression affects approximately 280 million people globally (WHO 2023); bipolar spectrum disorders affect roughly 1 in 100 people worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Women are 2x more likely to experience major depressive episodes than men; bipolar I is equally prevalent across sexes
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of onset for major depressive disorder is 32 years; bipolar I typically emerges in late adolescence or early 20s
keyStats.globalCases: Depression: ~280 million worldwide; bipolar spectrum: ~45 million globally; combined mood disorders account for the largest mental health disability burden

definition: Mood disorders (ICD-10: F30-F39) are a diagnostic class in the DSM-5 and ICD-11 characterized by a primary disturbance in mood as the core feature, accompanied by changes in activity, cognition, and psychomotor functioning. The DSM-5 distinguishes between depressive disorders (where the dominant mood is depressed or irritable, without a history of manic or hypomanic episodes) and bipolar and related disorders (where the individual has experienced at least one manic or hypomanic episode, often alternating with depressive episodes). This distinction is clinically critical: antidepressants given without mood stabilization to someone with undiagnosed bipolar disorder can trigger manic switching, rapid cycling, or mixed episodes. The overall category also includes premenstrual dysphoric disorder, persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia), disruptive mood dysregulation disorder in children, and substance-induced or medical-condition-related mood disorders. The boundary between normal emotional variation and a diagnosable mood disorder is set by the presence of significant functional impairment, symptom duration, and the exclusion of other causes including general medical conditions and substances.

typesClassification.description: One or more major depressive episodes without any history of mania or hypomania. Characterized by depressed mood or anhedonia for at least 2 weeks, plus at least four additional neurovegetative symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy, cognition, suicidality). Must cause clinically significant distress or impairment.

Depressed mood present for most days for at least 2 years (1 year in children), with no more than 2 months free of symptoms. Fewer or less severe symptoms than MDD, but the chronicity produces substantial cumulative impairment.

At least one manic episode lasting 7+ days (or any duration requiring hospitalization), usually alternating with major depressive episodes. Full manic episodes cause marked impairment; mixed episodes are possible. Lifetime prevalence approximately 0.6-1%.

At least one hypomanic episode (elevated or irritable mood, 4+ days) and at least one major depressive episode. No full manic episodes. Hypomanic symptoms cause observable change but not the marked impairment of mania.
Numerous periods of hypomanic and depressive symptoms lasting at least 2 years (1 year in children), never meeting criteria for MDD, hypomania, or mania. Chronic, fluctuating, and often unrecognized.

A specifier of MDD or, less commonly, bipolar disorder with a seasonal pattern: depressive episodes recur predictably in winter or fall, with full remission or switching to hypomania/mania in spring/summer. Linked to circadian rhythm disruption and reduced serotonin activity in winter.

Severe, cyclical luteal-phase mood instability (irritability, dysphoria, anxiety, lethargy) causing significant impairment. Distinct from PMS; requires prospective daily charting confirming symptom pattern over two cycles.

Severe, recurrent temper outbursts grossly out of proportion to the situation, with persistently irritable or angry mood between episodes, in children aged 6-18. A DSM-5 addition designed to reduce overdiagnosis of pediatric bipolar disorder.

A prominent, persistent mood disturbance directly caused by a substance (drug of abuse, medication, or toxin) with evidence that symptoms developed during or soon after exposure and have persisted beyond a reasonable withdrawal window.

primarySymptoms: Persistent sadness, emptiness, or tearfulness present most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks: the hallmark of a major depressive episode.
Marked loss of interest or pleasure in all or almost all activities (anhedonia), including hobbies, social life, food, and intimacy, that was not present before.
Significant weight loss or gain, or decrease or increase in appetite: defined as a change of more than 5% body weight in a month without intentional effort.
Insomnia (difficulty falling or staying asleep) or hypersomnia (excessive sleep) nearly every day, often with early-morning awakening and inability to return to sleep.
Psychomotor agitation or retardation observable by others: the person may be visibly restless, pacing, and unable to sit still, or conversely slowed in speech, movement, and expression.
Fatigue or loss of energy nearly every day, disproportionate to exertion, making routine tasks feel insurmountable.
Feelings of worthlessness or excessive, inappropriate guilt: persistent self-criticism, sense of being a burden, and guilt about things that are not the individual's fault.
Diminished ability to think, concentrate, or make decisions: patients often report their mind feels foggy, they lose focus reading or watching television, and they struggle with work tasks.
Recurrent thoughts of death, suicidal ideation, suicide attempts, or a specific plan for suicide: requires urgent clinical evaluation regardless of whether the person intends to act.
In a manic episode: abnormally elevated, expansive, or irritable mood plus goal-directed activity or psychomotor agitation lasting 7+ days (or requiring hospitalization).

In a hypomanic episode: a distinct period of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood and increased energy lasting at least 4 days, with changes in functioning observable by others but not severe enough to cause marked impairment.
Mixed episode features: simultaneous or rapidly alternating depressive and manic or hypomanic symptoms occurring within the same episode, carrying particularly high suicide risk.
earlyWarningSigns: A persistent shift in sleep pattern: sleeping significantly more or less than usual for two or more consecutive weeks without explanation
Withdrawal from friends, family, and previously enjoyed activities that was not preceded by a clear stressor or life change
Increased reliance on alcohol or other substances as a coping mechanism for low mood or anxiety
Difficulty concentrating on work or academic tasks that previously posed no challenge, especially when accompanied by subjective cognitive complaints
Changes in appetite and weight that represent a noticeable departure from the person's baseline, in either direction
Recurring pessimistic or hopeless thoughts about the future, especially when these feel intrusive or uncontrollable
A first-degree relative with a known mood disorder: the strongest single risk factor for developing one
A prior depressive or hypomanic episode, even if it resolved spontaneously: recurrence risk after a first episode is approximately 50%, rising with each subsequent episode
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation with a specific plan, means, or stated intent: requires immediate emergency psychiatric evaluation
Homicidal or violent ideation with intent or a plan: requires immediate safety planning and emergency referral
Severe manic episode with delusions, hallucinations, or severely impaired judgment: the person may be at risk for financial ruin, legal consequences, or dangerous behavior
Mixed episode with combined manic activation and depressive hopelessness: carries the highest suicide risk in bipolar disorder and requires urgent intervention
New onset of severe mood symptoms immediately postpartum in a new mother: postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency
Mood symptoms attributable to a medical cause (thyroid disorder, B12 deficiency, Cushing's, medications) requiring medical evaluation before psychiatric treatment

causes.description: Mood disorders are among the most heritable psychiatric conditions. Family studies show a 2-4x increased risk in first-degree relatives of individuals with MDD, and a 7-10x increased risk for bipolar I in first-degree relatives. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified dozens of polygenic risk variants with small individual effect sizes but cumulative impact. The heritability of MDD is estimated at 37-40%; for bipolar I it is higher at 60-80%.

Core neurobiological systems implicated include the serotonin system (regulating mood, sleep, and appetite), the norepinephrine-dopamine pathway (energy, motivation, reward), and the HPA axis (stress response and cortisol regulation). In depression, monoamine depletion and receptor downregulation are well-established; in bipolar disorder, membrane fatty acid metabolism, mitochondrial dysfunction, and calcium channel dysregulation are also implicated. Neuroimaging consistently shows altered activity in the prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus.

Adverse childhood experiences: physical, sexual, or emotional abuse, neglect, and household dysfunction are among the strongest predictors of adult mood disorders. The cumulative stress burden model shows a dose-response relationship between ACE score and depression risk. Adult-onset stressors (job loss, bereavement, divorce, trauma) precipitate episodes in vulnerable individuals through glucocorticoid-mediated effects on the hippocampus and amygdala.

Elevated peripheral cytokines (IL-6, CRP, TNF-alpha) are found in a substantial subset of patients with MDD and bipolar disorder. The 'cytokine hypothesis' proposes that chronic low-grade inflammation disrupts monoamine metabolism, impairs neuroplasticity, and activates the HPA axis, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of inflammation and mood dysregulation. Anti-inflammatory agents are under investigation as augmentation strategies.

Disruption of the body's intrinsic circadian clock: regulated by the suprachiasmatic nucleus and synchronized by light exposure, this is strongly implicated in SAD and appears to play a role in broader mood disorders. Evening-type chronotype, shift work, and jet lag are associated with increased mood disorder risk. Sleep-timing abnormalities are both a symptom and a contributing cause.

Alcohol is a depressogenic substance that both triggers and perpetuates mood episodes. Cannabis use is associated with earlier onset and worsened course of bipolar disorder. Certain medications: corticosteroids, interferon-alpha, some chemotherapeutic agents, mefloquine, and some beta-blockers can induce depressive or manic symptoms. Substance-induced mood disorder requires distinguishing between direct pharmacological effect and an independent primary mood disorder.

Hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, Addison's disease, vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, iron deficiency, HIV/AIDS, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, stroke, and certain cancers all present with prominent mood symptoms. Screening for these 'mimics' is a required step before assigning a primary psychiatric diagnosis.

riskFactors.description: Each prior episode increases recurrence risk by approximately 15-20%. Recurrence after two episodes is approximately 70-80%, and most clinicians recommend maintenance treatment after a third episode.
A first-degree relative with MDD increases personal risk 2-4x; with bipolar I, risk increases 7-10x.
Women are twice as likely to develop MDD across the lifespan, likely due to hormonal factors (postpartum, perimenopause), gender-based violence, and social stressors.
Four or more ACEs increase MDD odds by 4-5x. ACEs alter HPA axis programming and are associated with smaller hippocampal volume in adulthood.

Chronic pain, cancer, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and autoimmune conditions all increase mood disorder risk and worsen medical outcomes: the depression-medical comorbidity bidirectional relationship is well-documented.
Anxiety disorders co-occur in 50-60% of MDD cases and substantially worsen prognosis, treatment response, and functional outcome.
Alcohol use disorder and cannabis use disorder both precipitate mood episodes, worsen course, and complicate pharmacological treatment.
Insomnia is not only a symptom of mood disorders but an independent risk factor for developing them. Treating insomnia reduces subsequent MDD incidence.

Bereavement, divorce, job loss, and interpersonal conflict are precipitants in the majority of first depressive episodes. Not all stressors cause mood episodes: vulnerability and prior episodes determine who is susceptible.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and social isolation
riskFactors.description: Poverty, loneliness, and lack of social support are robust predictors of chronic mood disorders and barriers to treatment access. Social determinants of mental health are now central to public health framing.

affectedDemographics: Depression: approximately 5% of adults globally; 280 million people worldwide (WHO 2023)
Bipolar I and II combined: approximately 1% for bipolar I, 0.4-1.2% for bipolar II; relatively equal sex distribution
SAD affects approximately 1-3% of the general population, rising to 5-10% in primary care populations in higher latitudes
PMDD affects approximately 3-8% of menstruating women; widely underdiagnosed
Mood disorders are the leading cause of disability worldwide among adults aged 15-44 (WHO Global Burden of Disease)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of mood disorders is clinical, based on structured criteria from the DSM-5 or ICD-11. No single laboratory test confirms major depression or bipolar disorder; diagnosis rests on a thorough clinical interview covering symptom type, duration, frequency, severity, and functional impact, supplemented by standardized rating scales (PHQ-9 for depression; Young Mania Rating Scale for mania; Altman Self-Rating Mania Scale). The clinical interview should systematically assess: current mood state and any past manic or hypomanic episodes (the most critical distinction in the bipolar spectrum); suicide risk and safety; psychiatric comorbidity including anxiety, substance use, and personality disorders; medical history including thyroid function, metabolic panel, B12, folate, and HIV/hepatitis screening; current medications and substance use; family history of mood disorder, suicide, and psychiatric hospitalization; and psychosocial stressors and protective factors. The assessment should rule out medical mimics: hypothyroidism, hypercortisolism, B12 deficiency before assigning a primary psychiatric diagnosis. For bipolar disorder, the challenge is identifying past hypomanic episodes that the patient may not have recognized as pathological. Structured instruments such as the Mood Disorder Questionnaire (MDQ) and the Hypomania Checklist (HCL-32) are useful screening adjuncts. Importantly, a single adequate trial of an antidepressant at therapeutic dose for 6-8 weeks should be considered before concluding that a patient has treatment-resistant illness.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard structured diagnostic interview for mood disorders. Distinguishes MDD from bipolar disorder, identifies psychiatric comorbidity, and establishes severity and functional impairment. Takes 60-90 minutes in its full form; brief structured assessment (MINI) is adequate for most clinical settings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician asks about current and past mood episodes, sleep, appetite, energy, suicidality, psychotic symptoms, and manic features. There are no needles or scanners: it is a conversational assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A 9-item self-report tool for screening and monitoring depression severity. Scores range from 0-27; a score of 10 or above suggests probable major depression and warrants clinical follow-up. Useful for tracking response to treatment over time.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A paper or digital questionnaire completed in 5 minutes. Results are scored immediately and reviewed during the clinical encounter.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Clinician-rated 11-item scale for assessing manic symptom severity. Scores above 20 suggest moderate-to-severe mania. Used to establish baseline severity before treatment and to monitor response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Completed by a clinician based on a brief interview (10-15 minutes) covering mood, activity, sleep need, speech, thought content, and insight.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism both produce mood symptoms that can mimic depression, bipolar, or anxiety. TSH is the single most important screening test before assigning any primary psychiatric diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results available within hours. Most labs include TSH in a basic metabolic panel or thyroid panel.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, metabolic panel, B12, folate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out anemia, renal/hepatic dysfunction, and B12/folate deficiency: all of which produce depressive symptoms. These are the most common reversible medical causes of mood symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood panel; usually drawn alongside thyroid screening for efficiency.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies substance use that may be driving or contributing to mood symptoms. Particularly important before initiating pharmacotherapy, as many mood medications interact dangerously with alcohol, stimulants, or opioids.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard urine collection. Results typically available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Neuroimaging (MRI or CT) if focal neurological signs present
diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routinely indicated for mood disorder diagnosis. Ordered when history or examination suggests a structural brain lesion, stroke, tumor, or demyelinating disease as a cause of mood symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI preferred; CT more accessible. Takes 20-45 minutes. No radiation with MRI.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of mood disorders is stratified by diagnosis, severity, chronicity, and patient preference. For mild-to-moderate major depressive disorder, structured psychotherapies: particularly Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) and Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy (IPSRT) are first-line as monotherapy. For moderate-to-severe MDD, pharmacotherapy with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), or bupropion is the standard initial approach, with psychotherapy added for combination treatment. For bipolar disorder, mood stabilizers: lithium, valproate, lamotrigine, or carbamazepine are the cornerstone; antidepressants are used only as augmentation and never as monotherapy because of the risk of switching into mania or accelerating cycling. For SAD, light therapy (10,000 lux, 30 minutes each morning) is first-line, often combined with CBT. Across all conditions, treatment adherence, psychoeducation, sleep hygiene, social support, and management of comorbidities are universal. Treatment-resistant depression (failure of two adequate antidepressant trials) warrants specialist evaluation, may benefit from augmentation strategies (aripiprazole, quetiapine, bupropion, lithium augmentation), and in refractory cases, transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), intravenous ketamine/esketamine, or electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) may be indicated.

medicalTreatments.name: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antidepressants for MDD, panic disorder, and anxiety comorbidity. Inhibit serotonin reuptake with relatively favorable side effect profiles. Effective for mild-to-moderate depression in 50-65% of patients. In bipolar disorder, SSRIs can trigger manic switching: use only with concurrent mood stabilizer.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 50-65% in MDD; remission rates approximately 35-45% after 8-10 weeks at adequate dose.

medicalTreatments.name: SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine, desvenlafaxine)
medicalTreatments.description: Dual serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors. Slightly more noradrenergic than SSRIs; duloxetine has evidence for comorbid chronic pain. Comparable efficacy to SSRIs but with a slightly different side effect profile including more nausea and hypertension at higher doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates comparable to SSRIs at 50-65%; some evidence of superior efficacy in severe depression.

medicalTreatments.name: Bupropion (Wellbutrin)
medicalTreatments.description: Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor with no serotonergic activity. Particularly useful when sexual side effects, sedation, or weight gain from SSRIs are problematic. Contraindicated in seizure disorder, eating disorders, and abrupt alcohol or benzodiazepine discontinuation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable antidepressant efficacy to SSRIs; superior adherence due to once-daily dosing and favorable side effect profile for some patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Lithium (Eskalith, Lithobid)
medicalTreatments.description: The gold-standard mood stabilizer for bipolar I disorder. Reduces manic and depressive relapse by approximately 40-60%. At serum levels above 0.6 mEq/L, has specific antisuicidal properties: the only antidepressant class with demonstrated suicide risk reduction independent of mood effect. Requires regular renal function and thyroid monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces suicide attempts and completion in bipolar disorder by 5-7x compared to other mood stabilizers; reduces relapse in unipolar depression as an augmentation agent.

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine (Lamictal)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line mood stabilizer with particular efficacy in preventing bipolar depression. Has minimal or no effect on manic episodes. Requires slow titration (12.5-25 mg weekly increases) due to Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk in the first 8 weeks. Particularly valuable in bipolar II depression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents depressive relapse in bipolar I and II with 30-40% reduction in depressive episode frequency. Less effective for acute depression but excellent for maintenance.

medicalTreatments.name: Valproate (Depakote)
medicalTreatments.description: Mood stabilizer effective for acute mania and mixed episodes. Less evidence for bipolar depression prevention. Teratogenic (neural tube defects); avoid in women of childbearing potential unless on reliable contraception. Weight gain, hair loss, and tremor are common side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls acute mania in 50-60% of patients; less effective for long-term bipolar depression prevention than lithium or lamotrigine.

medicalTreatments.name: Atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole, lurasidone)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as adjunctive treatment in MDD (quetiapine, aripiprazole), acute mania (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine), and bipolar depression (lurasidone, quetiapine, olanzapine-fluoxetine combination). Carry metabolic risks (weight, glucose, lipids) that require monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Quetiapine XR has FDA approval for MDD augmentation; lurasidone for bipolar depression; all are effective for acute mania with NNT approximately 3-5 versus placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Ketamine / Esketamine (Spravato)
medicalTreatments.description: NMDA receptor antagonist (racemic ketamine IV; intranasal esketamine). Produces rapid antidepressant effects within hours in treatment-resistant depression and bipolar depression. Esketamine is FDA-approved for TRD and bipolar depression with risk of suicidal ideation. Requires supervised administration in a certified clinic.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 50-70% in treatment-resistant depression within 24 hours; durability of effect requires repeated dosing.

surgicalOptions.name: Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT)
surgicalOptions.description: The most effective somatic treatment for severe, treatment-resistant, or psychotic depression, catatonia, and acute mania when rapid control is needed. Works via generalized seizure induction under anesthesia; mechanism thought to involve neuroplasticity, HPA axis normalization, and global anticonvulsant effects. Bilateral placement preferred for depression; unilateral allows less memory impairment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates 80-90% in severe treatment-resistant depression; superior to pharmacotherapy in psychotic depression, catatonia, and high suicide risk where speed is essential.

surgicalOptions.name: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS)
surgicalOptions.description: Non-invasive neuromodulation using magnetic pulses to stimulate the left prefrontal cortex for depression. FDA-approved for MDD and as an augmentation strategy. Standard course is 5 sessions per week for 4-6 weeks. Particularly useful for patients who cannot tolerate or refuse ECT.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response rates approximately 50-60% in treatment-resistant depression; remission rates 30-40%. Higher with accelerated or theta-burst protocols.

surgicalOptions.name: Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgically implanted device delivering pulsed electrical stimulation to the left vagus nerve. FDA-approved for treatment-resistant depression. Effects build over 12-24 months. Reserved for patients who have failed multiple antidepressant trials and ECT.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term response rates 30-40% at 12 months in treatment-resistant depression; benefits accumulate over years of stimulation.

surgicalOptions.name: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS): investigational
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical implantation of electrodes in specific brain regions (subcallosal cingulate, ventral capsule/ventral striatum) under investigation for severe treatment-resistant depression. Not FDA-approved; available through research protocols at academic centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Investigational; early trials show response rates of 40-60% at 12 months in carefully selected patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bright light therapy (10,000 lux, 30 min morning)
alternativeTreatments.description: First-line treatment for Seasonal Affective Disorder with response rates comparable to antidepressants (50-70%). Mechanism involves circadian rhythm entrainment and serotonergic modulation. Also reduces non-seasonal depression severity when used as augmentation. Use filtered lamps to avoid retinal damage.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured 12-20 session psychotherapy targeting maladaptive thought patterns and behavioral avoidance. Equivalent efficacy to antidepressants in mild-to-moderate MDD; superior for preventing relapse because skills are internalized. Adapted for bipolar disorder as CBT-BP (with relapse prevention focus).

alternativeTreatments.name: Interpersonal and Social Rhythm Therapy (IPSRT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Developed for bipolar disorder; focuses on stabilizing daily rhythms (sleep, meals, activity) as the primary intervention for preventing relapse. Evidence-based; reduces time to remission and increases inter-episode stability.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (EPA 1-2 g/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Modest evidence for EPA-dominant omega-3 augmentation in MDD, particularly in patients with elevated inflammatory markers. The Cochrane meta-analysis found a number needed to treat of 13: meaningful for natural-medicine preference populations but inferior to standard pharmacotherapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Exercise (moderate-intensity aerobic, 150 min/week)
alternativeTreatments.description: Equivalent to antidepressants in mild-to-moderate depression per meta-analyses (Schuch et al., 2018). Mechanism involves endorphin release, HPA axis normalization, BDNF up-regulation, and anti-inflammatory effects. Adherence is the primary limitation in clinical practice.

whySeeSpecialist: A psychiatrist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain between unipolar and bipolar depression, when the patient has psychotic features, when two or more adequate antidepressant trials have failed, when manic or hypomanic symptoms are present or suspected, when the patient is actively suicidal or has made a suicide attempt, when the patient is pregnant or breastfeeding and needs mood-stabilizer safety planning, or when complex comorbidity with substance use, personality disorder, or anxiety requires integrated specialist management. Primary care manages mild, uncomplicated major depression effectively with SSRIs and referral for psychotherapy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in psychiatry, experience with mood disorder diagnosis and treatment across the bipolar-depression spectrum, and willingness to distinguish unipolar from bipolar presentations using systematic assessment tools. Ask whether the clinician incorporates psychotherapy referrals into the treatment plan and whether they monitor treatment response using validated scales (PHQ-9, YMRS) at follow-up visits. For bipolar disorder specifically, ask about the clinician's approach to antidepressant use, as this is where most treatment errors occur. Continuity matters: mood disorder management is a long-term relationship.
preventionStrategies: Maintain stable daily rhythms: consistent sleep-wake times, meal times, and activity schedules are the single most evidence-based behavioral intervention for preventing bipolar relapse
Engage in regular exercise (150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity): reduces subsequent MDD incidence by approximately 25-30%
Limit or eliminate alcohol: which is depressogenic and disrupts sleep architecture. Even moderate drinking increases depressive episode risk in vulnerable individuals
Treat insomnia promptly: insomnia is an independent risk factor for new mood episodes and should be addressed before it triggers a full episode
Maintain social connections: loneliness and social isolation are among the most robust predictors of chronic depression and are modifiable risk factors

Monitor for early warning signs with a written relapse prevention plan developed collaboratively with the treating clinician: identifying prodromal symptoms and early interventions reduces episode severity and duration
For women with a history of postpartum mood episodes, consider perinatal psychiatric consultation for prophylactic treatment planning before next pregnancy
lifestyleModifications: Adopt a structured daily routine with fixed wake time, meal times, and sleep time: consistency in social rhythms is protective against bipolar relapse and improves depression outcomes
Reduce screen exposure in the evening and maintain a dark, cool sleep environment: circadian hygiene directly impacts mood regulation
Engage in at least 20 minutes of morning outdoor light exposure daily, particularly in winter months and if there is a history of SAD
Limit or eliminate alcohol consumption: it is a central nervous system depressant that disrupts sleep architecture and interferes with mood stabilizers
Build and maintain a social support network: regular social contact is protective against depressive relapse
Keep a mood chart or use a mood tracking app: patterns often emerge that help the patient and clinician intervene earlier in subsequent episodes
Develop a crisis plan that includes emergency contacts, current medications, and what to do if suicidal ideation emerges: written plans reduce the impulse to act in crisis

recommended: Omega-3 rich foods (salmon, sardines, mackerel, flaxseeds, walnuts): EPA and DHA support membrane fatty acid metabolism implicated in mood regulation
B-vitamin adequate diet (leafy greens, legumes, fortified grains): B12 and folate deficiencies are linked to depression; maintain adequate levels
Mediterranean-style dietary pattern: adherence is associated with 25-30% lower depression incidence in large cohort studies
Magnesium-rich foods (pumpkin seeds, spinach, dark chocolate): magnesium has mild anxiolytic and antidepressant properties
Fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, kimchi, sauerkraut): emerging evidence links gut microbiome composition to mood regulation via the gut-brain axis
Consistent meal timing aligned with circadian rhythm: irregular eating patterns disrupt the suprachiasmatic nucleus clock
avoid: Alcohol in any quantity if in a depressive or manic episode, or during active treatment: it is a depressogenic substance that disrupts medication metabolism
Processed foods, refined carbohydrates, and high-glycemic-index foods: associated with increased depression risk in large epidemiological studies
Excessive caffeine in bipolar disorder: it can exacerbate anxiety, trigger sleep disruption, and in some cases contribute to manic symptoms
Very restrictive diets that eliminate entire food groups: nutritional deficiencies can worsen mood, energy, and cognitive function
High-sodium foods if on lithium: sodium depletion increases lithium reabsorption and raises serum lithium levels, requiring consistent hydration and dietary sodium

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week at moderate intensity) is an evidence-based antidepressant intervention for mild-to-moderate depression, with effect sizes comparable to antidepressants in head-to-head meta-analyses. Exercise works through BDNF upregulation (supporting hippocampal neuroplasticity), HPA axis normalization, monoamine augmentation, and anti-inflammatory effects. For bipolar disorder, structured, predictable exercise is preferable to high-intensity or irregular exercise, which can destabilize sleep-wake rhythms. During an active depressive or manic episode, exercise is often not possible or desirable: during these phases, medical treatment takes priority and exercise is introduced once acute symptoms are under control.
dailyManagement: Take mood stabilizer and antidepressant medications at the same time each day: adherence is the strongest predictor of long-term stability in both unipolar and bipolar disorder
Track mood, sleep, and energy in a brief daily log or app: patterns across 4-8 weeks are more informative than a single snapshot for adjusting treatment
Maintain consistent wake and sleep times even on weekends: circadian stability is protective and prevents triggering depressive or hypomanic episodes
Recheck serum lithium and thyroid levels every 6 months once stable: lithium requires ongoing monitoring of renal function and thyroid status
Keep a current medication list accessible: including doses and timing for use in emergencies or when seeing a new clinician
Identify and act on early warning signs using a written relapse prevention plan: most episodes have prodromal patterns that, if caught early, can be addressed with brief interventions
prognosis: Major depressive disorder has a favorable prognosis with appropriate treatment: 70-80% of patients respond to adequate antidepressant pharmacotherapy or psychotherapy, and 50-60% achieve remission. Without treatment, episodes typically resolve over 6-12 months but recurrence risk is high (50% after one episode, rising to 80-90% after three). Chronic dysthymia has a worse functional outcome than recurrent MDD and often requires combination pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy. Bipolar I disorder is a lifelong condition with a relapsing-remitting course. Lithium reduces manic relapse by approximately 40% and substantially reduces suicide risk, but many patients require combination treatment. The long-term functional outcome for bipolar disorder is variable: roughly 30-40% of patients achieve full functional recovery between episodes, 30-40% have persistent subthreshold symptoms, and 20-30% have persistent impairment. Key prognostic factors include: early intervention, treatment adherence, absence of substance use comorbidity, stable social rhythm, and strong social support.
recoveryTimeline: Acute antidepressant response typically emerges over 2-4 weeks; full therapeutic effect is achieved at 6-8 weeks. In major depression, remission of core mood symptoms often precedes resolution of neurovegetative symptoms (sleep, appetite, energy) by several weeks: clinicians and patients should avoid stopping medication prematurely before full effect is achieved. In bipolar disorder, acute mania is controlled over 2-4 weeks with mood stabilizers and atypical antipsychotics. Depressive episodes in bipolar take longer to resolve than manic episodes: typically 8-12 weeks with appropriate treatment. Lithium's antisuicidal effect is achieved rapidly (within days) while its mood-stabilizing relapse prevention effect builds over 6-12 months of continuous treatment. Maintenance treatment is recommended for a minimum of 6-12 months after remission from a first depressive episode, and indefinitely after two or more episodes.
complications: Treatment-resistant depression: defined as failure to respond to two adequate antidepressant trials, affects 20-30% of patients and requires specialist management with augmentation, combination, or neuromodulation strategies
Bipolar disorder progression: the condition tends to worsen over time without treatment, with episodes becoming more frequent, more severe, and more treatment-resistant (kindling model)

Substance use disorders co-occurring with mood disorders: affect 30-50% of bipolar patients and substantially worsen outcomes, increase suicide risk, and complicate pharmacotherapy due to drug interactions

Suicide and suicidal behavior: approximately 15% of patients with severe mood disorders die by suicide; attempted suicide occurs in 20-30% of bipolar patients and 10-15% of MDD patients over the lifespan

Cardiovascular disease: depression and bipolar disorder are independent risk factors for coronary artery disease and stroke, mediated by HPA axis dysregulation, inflammatory activation, and lifestyle factors (sedentary behavior, smoking, medication side effects)

Financial, legal, and occupational consequences from manic episodes: spending sprees, legal issues from disinhibited behavior, job loss from impaired judgment are common in bipolar I and carry long-term consequences even after mood stabilizes
Postpartum mood episodes: women with a history of bipolar or major depression have a 30-50% risk of a postpartum mood episode and require perinatal psychiatric planning

supportResources.name: National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH): Mood Disorders
supportResources.description: Authoritative US government resource covering symptoms, treatment, and research updates for depression, bipolar disorder, and related conditions.

supportResources.name: Depression and Bipolar Support Alliance (DBSA)
supportResources.description: Peer-led support groups, wellness tools, and educational resources for people living with mood disorders. One of the largest consumer-run organizations in the mental health space.

supportResources.name: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5
supportResources.description: The definitive diagnostic manual for mood disorders. APA also publishes patient-facing summaries of DSM-5 criteria.

supportResources.name: WHO Mental Health Gap Action Programme (mhGAP)
supportResources.description: Global WHO resource for scaling up care for mood and other mental disorders in low- and middle-income settings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anxiety Disorders
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anxiety disorders present with excessive fear and worry as the primary disturbance, not depressed mood or anhedonia. Panic disorder, social anxiety, and generalized anxiety can be comorbid with mood disorders, but the anxiety presentation comes first. Some patients present with 'anxious depression': MDD with prominent anxiety comorbidity, which requires integrated treatment addressing both dimensions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PTSD involves re-experiencing a past trauma (intrusive memories, flashbacks, nightmares), hypervigilance, and avoidance of trauma reminders. Mood symptoms in PTSD are prominent but secondary to the trauma-related symptom cluster. The trauma must have involved actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence per DSM-5 criteria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Borderline Personality Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Borderline personality disorder presents with unstable interpersonal relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, and chronic emptiness. Mood instability in BPD is rapid (hours to days) and triggered by interpersonal events, whereas mood episodes in bipolar disorder last days to weeks and are not primarily interpersonal in origin. BPD is treated with dialectical behavior therapy (DBT); mood disorders are not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schizophrenia Spectrum Disorders
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schizophrenia presents with delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized speech as the primary disturbance. Mood episodes can occur but are secondary to the psychotic symptoms, which are present even outside mood episodes. The key distinction is the presence of psychotic symptoms in the absence of a major mood episode, or the prominence of psychotic symptoms relative to mood symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
relatedConditions.relevance: MDD is the most common condition within the mood disorder spectrum: a primary depressive disorder without manic episodes. Mood-disorder.json serves as the parent page covering MDD, bipolar spectrum, dysthymia, SAD, PMDD, and DMDD.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Bipolar I and II are the conditions on the opposite pole from unipolar depression in the mood disorder spectrum. Distinguishing unipolar from bipolar depression is the single most important diagnostic step in mood disorder assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia)
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic depressive disorder lasting at least 2 years: on the milder end of the depressive spectrum but with cumulative functional impairment comparable to recurrent MDD.

relatedConditions.name: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)
relatedConditions.relevance: A depression specifier with a predictable seasonal pattern: most relevant for patients in higher latitudes or those whose depressive episodes consistently track with winter months.

relatedConditions.name: Anxiety Disorders
relatedConditions.relevance: Anxiety and mood disorders share neurobiological pathways (HPA axis, serotonin, norepinephrine) and co-occur in the majority of patients. Treatment often addresses both simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
relatedConditions.relevance: PTSD and mood disorders share the HPA axis dysregulation and neurobiological burden of chronic stress. Comorbidity is high in clinical populations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a mood disorder and feeling sad?
faqs.answer: Normal sadness is a proportionate emotional response to a clear stressor: loss, disappointment, rejection that fades over days to weeks without significantly impairing daily functioning. A mood disorder is distinguished by: symptoms that persist beyond 2 weeks without a clear trigger, or symptoms that are grossly out of proportion to any precipitant; significant impairment in work, relationships, or self-care; and symptoms meeting specific DSM-5 criteria (such as depressed mood or anhedonia plus four additional neurovegetative symptoms). The presence of suicidal ideation, inability to function at work or home, or psychotic features are clear indicators that sadness has crossed into a diagnosable mood disorder.

faqs.question: What are the main types of mood disorders?
faqs.answer: The two broadest categories are depressive disorders and bipolar and related disorders. Depressive disorders include major depressive disorder (single or recurrent episodes), persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia), premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and disruptive mood dysregulation disorder in children. Bipolar and related disorders include bipolar I, bipolar II, cyclothymic disorder, and substance- or medical-condition-induced mood disorder. Seasonal affective disorder is a specifier of depression with a seasonal pattern. Each has distinct diagnostic criteria, treatment approaches, and natural histories.

faqs.question: How is major depressive disorder diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A clinician diagnoses MDD by conducting a structured interview covering the nine DSM-5 symptom criteria: depressed mood or anhedonia (at least one must be present), plus changes in sleep, appetite, energy, cognition, psychomotor activity, worthlessness/guilt, and suicidal ideation. At least five symptoms must be present nearly every day for 2 weeks, cause clinically significant distress or impairment, and not be attributable to substances or a medical condition. The clinician also rules out a history of manic or hypomanic episodes (which would indicate bipolar disorder) and ensures the symptoms are not better explained by schizophrenia or another psychotic disorder.

faqs.question: How is bipolar disorder different from depression?
faqs.answer: The critical distinction is the presence of manic or hypomanic episodes. In major depression, the mood state is consistently low or anhedonic without any history of elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with increased energy lasting 4 or more days (hypomania) or 7 or more days (mania). In bipolar disorder, the person has experienced at least one manic episode (bipolar I) or hypomanic episode (bipolar II). Some patients present in a depressive episode without a known history of manic symptoms: this is where careful history-taking about past periods of elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, increased goal-directed activity, and reckless behavior becomes critical. The distinction is clinically essential because antidepressants can trigger manic episodes in bipolar patients.

faqs.question: What does a manic episode feel like?
faqs.answer: People in a manic episode describe feeling invincible, bulletproof, and extraordinarily capable: euphoric, energetic, and creative at levels that are unsustainable. Thoughts race, speech is pressured and difficult to interrupt, sleep need drops dramatically (feeling rested on 2-3 hours), and judgment becomes impaired: spending sprees, risky sexual behavior, and ill-advised business decisions are common. Irritability is as common as euphoria, especially when others attempt to set limits. Psychotic features (delusions of grandeur, paranoid ideation) can emerge at the peak of a severe manic episode. Most patients lack insight into the severity of their symptoms during the episode and resist treatment until the crash into depression or the consequences become catastrophic.

faqs.question: Can you have depression and bipolar disorder at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes: and this is common. Most people with bipolar disorder spend more time in the depressive pole of the illness than in manic or hypomanic episodes, so depressive episodes in bipolar are often the more disabling phase of the illness. Approximately 50-60% of patients with bipolar disorder are in a depressive episode at any given point in time. The challenge is that bipolar depression is managed differently from unipolar depression: antidepressants require mood stabilizer cover and are less effective for bipolar depression than for unipolar depression. Lamotrigine, quetiapine, lurasidone, and olanzapine-fluoxetine combination have evidence for bipolar depression specifically.

faqs.question: What causes mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Mood disorders result from a complex interaction between genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. No single cause explains all cases. Genetic factors account for 37-80% of risk depending on the specific condition. Neurobiological contributors include altered serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine, and glutamate signaling, HPA axis hyperactivation, inflammatory cytokine elevation, and disrupted circadian rhythms. Psychosocial factors: adverse childhood experiences, chronic stress, social isolation, recent major loss interact with biological vulnerability to precipitate episodes. This biopsychosocial model guides treatment: biological interventions (medication, neuromodulation), psychological interventions (psychotherapy), and social interventions (housing, social support, lifestyle change) all address different components of the causal web.

faqs.question: Are mood disorders genetic?
faqs.answer: Mood disorders run in families. Having a first-degree relative with major depression approximately doubles your personal risk. Having a first-degree relative with bipolar I increases your risk by 7-10x. This does not mean mood disorders are purely genetic: they are not Mendelian conditions, but rather that heritable traits (neurobiology, HPA axis reactivity, circadian patterns) interact with environment and life experience. Large GWAS studies have identified dozens of genetic variants, each with very small individual effect sizes, that collectively influence risk. The inheritance pattern is best understood as polygenic: many genes of small effect together create vulnerability that environmental factors then activate.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for major depression?
faqs.answer: The best treatment depends on severity, patient preference, and prior treatment history. For mild-to-moderate depression, structured psychotherapies (CBT, interpersonal therapy, behavioral activation) and SSRIs are equally effective as first-line options. For moderate-to-severe depression, combination treatment (antidepressant plus psychotherapy) is superior to either alone. For treatment-resistant depression, augmentation with aripiprazole or quetiapine, switching to a different antidepressant class, or progressing to TMS, ketamine, or ECT may be necessary. The most common treatment error is stopping medication too early: 6-12 months of continuous treatment is the minimum after a first episode, and indefinite treatment is recommended after multiple recurrences.

faqs.question: Do antidepressants cause suicide?
faqs.answer: Antidepressants do not cause suicide per se, but they can increase suicidal ideation in a small subset of patients: particularly adolescents and young adults under 25, during the first 2-4 weeks of treatment. This risk is likely related to the activation of suicidal thoughts before mood begins to improve. Clinical guidelines require that patients starting antidepressants be monitored closely during the initial weeks, with clear safety planning and easy access to the clinician. The FDA black-box warning on antidepressants for pediatric and young adult suicidality has been controversial: it reduced antidepressant prescribing, which some analyses link to increased suicide rates. The net clinical benefit of appropriate antidepressant use remains strongly positive; the small activation risk is manageable with monitoring.

faqs.question: Is lithium safe for long-term use?
faqs.answer: Lithium is one of the most thoroughly studied psychiatric medications, with a 70-year track record. Long-term lithium therapy is safe when serum levels are maintained in the therapeutic range (0.6-1.0 mEq/L for maintenance; up to 1.2 mEq/L for acute management) and renal function and thyroid status are monitored every 6 months. The main risks: hypothyroidism (10-20% of patients), reduced renal concentrating ability, and benign microscopic kidney changes in a subset of long-term users are generally manageable. The antisuicidal benefit of lithium is unique and well-documented. The most important determinant of lithium safety is adherence to monitoring and consistent hydration and sodium intake.

faqs.question: How long does it take for antidepressants to work?
faqs.answer: Antidepressants typically begin to show therapeutic effect within 1-2 weeks, with full clinical response at 6-8 weeks. Patients and clinicians should expect that sleep, appetite, and anxiety symptoms often improve before mood and energy. The most common reason for apparent treatment failure is stopping medication before adequate time has passed. Some patients require 10-12 weeks at an adequate dose before concluding that a particular agent has failed. If there is no response at all by week 4, dose escalation or a switch to a different agent is appropriate. If partial response is seen by week 6-8, augmentation or dose optimization is preferred over switching.

faqs.question: What is seasonal affective disorder and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: SAD is a specifier of major depressive disorder (or, less commonly, bipolar disorder) in which depressive episodes follow a predictable seasonal pattern: typically beginning in late fall or early winter and remitting in spring or early summer. It is linked to reduced daylight exposure disrupting the suprachiasmatic nucleus circadian clock and reducing serotonergic activity. First-line treatment is bright light therapy: 10,000 lux exposure for 30 minutes each morning, using a certified light therapy lamp at approximately 18 inches from the face. Antidepressants (particularly bupropion, which is also FDA-approved for SAD prevention) and CBT adapted for seasonal patterns are also effective. Most patients with SAD do not develop manic or hypomanic episodes in summer: the summer 'high' is more often euthymia with improved energy.

faqs.question: Can diet and exercise help with mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Yes: and the evidence is robust. The Mediterranean dietary pattern is associated with a 25-30% lower incidence of depression in large prospective cohort studies. Aerobic exercise at 150 minutes per week produces antidepressant effect sizes equivalent to antidepressants for mild-to-moderate depression in head-to-head meta-analyses. Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation (EPA 1-2 g/day) has modest but measurable benefit, particularly in patients with elevated inflammatory markers. Neither diet nor exercise replaces pharmacotherapy or psychotherapy for moderate-to-severe mood disorders, but both are valuable adjuncts that patients can implement independently. The primary barrier is motivation: the same neurobiological changes that produce depression also reduce volition, making it hard for patients to initiate these behaviors without support.

faqs.question: When should someone with a mood disorder go to the emergency room?
faqs.answer: Go to an emergency room or call emergency services immediately for: active suicidal ideation with a specific plan and means; suicide attempt or self-harm requiring medical attention; severe manic episode with psychotic features, impaired judgment, or inability to care for basic needs; mixed episode (combined manic activation and suicidal depression) which carries the highest suicide risk; and any situation where the person is a danger to themselves or others. Emergency rooms can provide crisis stabilization, medication adjustment, and psychiatric admission when necessary. For non-emergent but urgent concerns: a medication adjustment, worsening symptoms, or a question about whether to increase medication, contact the outpatient prescribing clinician the same day.

faqs.question: Can children and teenagers get mood disorders?
faqs.answer: Yes. Major depressive disorder can be diagnosed in children as young as 6 years old, though the presentation may differ from adults: irritable mood may be more prominent than depressed mood in children, and somatic complaints (headaches, stomach aches) often present as the chief complaint. Disruptive mood dysregulation disorder (DMDD) was added to DSM-5 specifically to address severe, chronic irritability in children aged 6-18 that was previously overdiagnosed as childhood bipolar disorder. Bipolar I disorder is rarely diagnosed before age 12 but becomes more common in mid-to-late adolescence. Treatment in children and adolescents differs from adults: SSRIs require careful monitoring for activation, and psychotherapy approaches are developmentally tailored.

faqs.question: What is the connection between mood disorders and substance use?
faqs.answer: The relationship is bidirectional and mutually reinforcing. Alcohol is a depressogenic substance: chronic use depletes serotonin and norepinephrine, disrupts sleep architecture, impairs judgment, and precipitates depressive episodes. Cannabis use is associated with earlier onset and worsened course of bipolar disorder and can trigger manic switching. Stimulant use (cocaine, methamphetamine) can precipitate manic episodes in bipolar patients. Conversely, people with mood disorders are more likely to self-medicate with alcohol or drugs to manage mood symptoms. Approximately 30-50% of patients with bipolar disorder have a comorbid substance use disorder at some point in their lives, making integrated treatment essential. Substance use treatment must occur concurrently with mood disorder treatment for either to be effective.

faqs.question: What is the link between mood disorders and physical health?
faqs.answer: Depression and bipolar disorder are associated with a 40-60% increased risk of cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and early mortality compared to age-matched controls. The mechanisms are multifactorial: HPA axis dysregulation and chronic inflammation (shared drivers of both mood disorders and metabolic disease), medication side effects (weight gain, dyslipidemia from atypical antipsychotics), lifestyle factors (sedentary behavior, poor nutrition, smoking), and reduced healthcare access due to mood symptoms. The WHO identifies physical health screening as an essential component of mood disorder management. Conversely, chronic medical conditions: cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, chronic pain increase depression risk 2-3x, creating a bidirectional burden. Integrated care addressing both mental and physical health outcomes is the recommended model.

faqs.question: Do mood disorders ever go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Mild depressive episodes sometimes resolve within 2-3 months without formal treatment, particularly when the precipitant is time-limited. However, untreated mood disorders carry significant risks: episode recurrence (50% after a first episode, rising with each recurrence), functional deterioration, substance use as self-medication, and for bipolar disorder, the risk of kindling: the tendency for episodes to become more frequent, more severe, and more treatment-resistant over time. Early treatment of a first episode does not just relieve current suffering: it reduces recurrence risk and may alter the long-term trajectory of the illness. Waiting for an episode to resolve on its own is a reasonable option only for mild, clearly precipitated, single-episode depression without prior history.

faqs.question: How do I help a loved one with a mood disorder?
faqs.answer: Start by educating yourself about the specific condition: understanding that depression is not laziness or weakness, and that mania is not just 'high energy' but a potentially dangerous brain state, is foundational. Encourage treatment without coercing: express concern, share specific observations, and offer to help find a clinician. During a depressive episode, gentle outreach (a text, an offer to walk together, a prepared meal) is more helpful than advice to 'snap out of it.' During a manic episode, focus on safety: restrict access to financial resources, car keys, and credit cards, and contact the clinician or crisis line. Attend family therapy or support groups (DBSA offers peer groups) to develop skills for managing the illness as a family. Take care of your own mental health: caregiver burnout is common and does not serve anyone.

faqs.question: What is the difference between mood disorder treatment in therapy versus medication?
faqs.answer: Psychotherapy (particularly CBT, behavioral activation, and interpersonal therapy) and pharmacotherapy (SSRIs, SNRIs, mood stabilizers) work through different mechanisms and are suited to different patient needs. Mild-to-moderate depression responds equally well to either as first-line treatment. Moderate-to-severe depression and bipolar disorder typically require combination treatment. Psychotherapy builds lasting skills (cognitive restructuring, behavioral activation, social rhythm management) that reduce relapse risk after treatment ends: pharmacotherapy does not teach skills but requires ongoing adherence to prevent relapse. Many patients prefer to start with psychotherapy and add medication only if needed; others prefer the simplicity of medication. Both approaches are valid, and combined treatment is the evidence-based standard for most moderate-to-severe presentations.

metaTitle: Mood Disorders: Symptoms, Types & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Mood disorders span depression and bipolar disorder. Expert-reviewed guide to symptoms, types, causes, diagnosis, and treatment options.

images.altText: Medical illustration showing the areas of the brain involved in mood regulation and depression
images.caption: Mood disorders involve dysregulation in brain circuits connecting the prefrontal cortex, amygdala, and hippocampus.

images.altText: Person journaling in a reflective setting representing mood self-monitoring and mood tracking
images.caption: Mood tracking through journaling or apps helps patients and clinicians identify patterns and early warning signs before full episodes develop.

images.altText: Illustration showing cognitive behavioral therapy techniques for mood disorders including thought reframing
images.caption: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is an evidence-based first-line treatment for depression and an effective adjunct for bipolar disorder management.

images.altText: PHQ-9 screening questionnaire for depression shown on a clipboard
images.caption: The PHQ-9 is a validated 9-item self-report tool for screening, diagnosing, and monitoring depression severity.
